reasoning: The deadline for Israel's Counter-Memorial has been officially extended to March 12, 2026. Following the submission of the Counter-Memorial, the ICJ typically allows time for subsequent written pleadings (Reply and Rejoinder) if requested, followed by oral arguments, and then a lengthy deliberation period of at least 6-12 months. Furthermore, Israel is expected to file preliminary objections (if they haven't already), which would further pause the merits proceedings until those jurisdictional questions are resolved. Based on the opinions of international law experts monitoring the case, preliminary objections might not even reach oral arguments until late 2026 or early 2027, making a final merits judgment before January 1, 2027 procedurally and practically impossible. The community prediction of 25% appears to significantly underestimate typical structural delays in ICJ proceedings. There is virtually a 0% chance of a definitive ruling finding Israel guilty in this case before 2027.
